GB1-W13-A3 - Kimura from Closed Guard.md.body:
## Concept

# Kimura from Closed Guard

**Position:** Closed Guard  
**Category:** Submission  
**Curriculum:** GB1 Week 13 A3  
**Confidence:** 4/5

The Kimura (figure-four shoulder lock) from closed guard is a high-percentage follow-up submission when the opponent defends the triangle or X-Collar by posting or reaching. It is a core component of the closed guard attack and retention system taught in GB1.

GB affiliate instruction (Gracie Barra Scottsdale, Week 13) emphasizes isolating the wrist first, forming a tight figure-four with forearm pressure, and using hip rotation/walking to generate torque rather than arm strength alone.

## Setup

Closed guard. Opponent has posture or is attempting to posture up / stack / post an arm in defense.

- Isolate one arm (commonly the far arm as they try to create space or defend a triangle threat).
- Secure strong wrist control before committing to the figure-four.

Wikilink connections:
- Direct follow-up / transition from [[GB1-W09-B3 - Triangle from Closed Guard]] and the [[2026-04-22 Counter to Triangle Stack Escape — Roy Dean]] (Kimura is the explicit reaction when opponent posts the arm during stack defense).
- Grip isolation concepts overlap with [[GB1-W01-B3 - X-Collar Choke (Thumb-In)]] (deep feed + control before finish) and Armbar from Guard finishes.

## Execution

### Isolate & Control the Wrist
1. As the opponent postures or reaches (common reaction to triangle or collar threat), secure a strong grip on their wrist. Thumb position should prevent them from easily turning the palm up or rotating the arm out.
2. Keep your grip tight and high on the forearm/wrist — do not let them pull the arm back.

### Form the Figure-Four
1. Bring your other hand to your own wrist, creating the classic Kimura figure-four (your forearm bone against their upper arm for maximum pressure).
2. Your choking-side elbow stays tight to your body initially.

### Break Posture + Apply Torque with Hips
1. Use your closed guard to elevate the opponent's hips and create an angle (shift your hips toward the side of the trapped arm).
2. Walk your hips/shoulders to the side while pulling their arm behind their back.
3. Apply shoulder pressure by rotating the figure-four. The torque comes primarily from your hip movement and leg squeeze, not pulling with the arms.
4. Finish by driving their hand toward their own back or the mat while maintaining the angle with your hips.

### Hand Placement & References
- **Wrist grip (critical first step)**: Thumb-up or wedge position that blocks palm turn. This is the same "control before figure" principle seen in the Roy Dean stack counter transition to Kimura.
- **Figure-four formation**: Forearm bone on their arm (not just hands). Your top hand grips your own wrist firmly.
- **Hip drive**: The closed guard leg on the figure-four side does the rotational work. See [[GB1-W09-B3 - Triangle from Closed Guard]] for the shared hip elevation and angle creation mechanics (GB Scottsdale "knee line" and posting hip base concepts apply here for stability during the walk).
- Compare to back control Kimuras and the wrist control in X-Collar Thumb-In finishes.

## Common Mistakes
- Forming the figure-four before solid wrist isolation (opponent turns palm and escapes or reverses).
- Relying on arm strength to finish instead of hip rotation and leg drive.
- Losing the angle by staying flat on your back — must walk hips to the side.
- Finishing in the wrong rotational direction (must take the arm behind their back, not across their body).

## Key Principles
- **Wrist control before figure-four**: Never lock the grip until you have the arm isolated and the palm controlled. This is the highest-percentage detail from GB affiliate Week 13 instruction.
- **Hips create the torque**: The closed guard and hip walk generate the rotational force; arms apply the finishing pressure. Directly aligns with GB "hips before hands" and the hip escape/angle mechanics reviewed in the GB Scottsdale triangle series.
- **Triangle defense = Kimura opportunity**: Opponents who defend the triangle by posting or reaching expose the exact arm needed for this attack. The two techniques are trained as a pair in GB1.
- **Angle > raw power**: Creating the side angle with your hips makes the shoulder lock tight even against larger opponents.
- **Forearm pressure over hand strength**: Using the bone of your forearm in the figure-four increases leverage and reduces grip fatigue.
- **Common reaction management**: When opponent turns the palm or posts, re-grip higher on the wrist and re-establish hip angle immediately — do not chase with arms only.

## Media & Visual References

**Primary (GB Affiliate — Prioritized per GB Greensboro rule):**
- Gracie Barra Scottsdale — "Kimura Attack from Closed Guard - Week 13" (https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=L3sPRsVbEa4). Exact GB1 Week 13 A3 curriculum video. Deep review of series context and available instruction confirms emphasis on wrist isolation first, tight figure-four with forearm pressure, and hip-driven torque/rotation. Why valuable: Provides the precise "control the wrist before figure-four" cue and hip walk detail that was previously generic in the card. Directly matches user's training at Gracie Barra Greensboro (same curriculum sequence).

- Gracie Barra Scottsdale Weekly Technique Videos playlist (https://www.youtube.com/playlist?list=PLr-z5vDCpY3L0Z4aj3Sy0pgVXQjhnAUkW) — full GB1-aligned affiliate series including Triangle from Closed Guard, Pendulum Sweep, and related closed guard attacks. GB Scottsdale content prioritized after Greensboro-specific public results were limited; all mechanics cross-checked for consistency with GB1 posture-breaking, hip elevation, and retention principles.

**Cross-references for triangle retention cluster (GB-aligned mechanics):**
- The hip elevation, angle creation, and posting hip base from the GB Scottsdale Triangle instruction (knee line high over shoulder; hip escape/shuffle to re-create angle under stack) apply directly to maintaining control during the Kimura hip walk.
- See [[2026-04-22 Counter to Triangle Stack Escape — Roy Dean]] for the live transition example from triangle threat → Kimura when opponent posts the arm.

**Recommended Photos / Diagrams**
- Close-up of wrist isolation grip (thumb position preventing palm turn) before figure-four.
- Figure-four formation with forearm bone pressure on the arm.
- Hip walk / angle creation moment showing the closed guard driving rotation.
- Finish with opponent's hand driven toward their back, shoulder pressure applied.

**Hand Placement & References**  
The wrist isolation + figure-four is the defining detail for this technique and transfers to many shoulder locks. See the Roy Dean stack counter card for the exact timing of the transition when the opponent defends the triangle by posting. The hip-driven torque concept is the same "angle > squeeze" principle reviewed in the GB Scottsdale triangle series and applies to [[GB1-W09-A3 - Armbar from Guard]] finishes as well.

GB1-W09-A2 - Pendulum Sweep.md.body:
## Concept

# Pendulum Sweep

**Position:** Closed Guard  
**Category:** Sweep  
**Curriculum:** GB1 Week 9 A2  
**Confidence:** 4/5

The pendulum sweep is a powerful closed guard sweep that uses hip elevation to create an angle combined with a wide pendulum-like leg swing for momentum. It is particularly effective when the opponent has one knee up or is postured in a way that creates a sweepable angle. GB1 Week 9 teaches it as part of the core closed guard sweep system alongside Scissor and other entries.

GB affiliate instruction (Gracie Barra Scottsdale, Week 9) highlights the importance of hip elevation first to load the sweep, the timing of the pendulum arc, and weight shift to the posting hip for base and power during the rotation.

## Setup

Closed guard. Opponent has base, often with one knee raised (creating the angle) or weight biased slightly to one side.

- Secure sleeve or collar control on the side toward which you will sweep.
- Your own hips must be active and ready to elevate.

Wikilink connections:
- Builds directly on the hip elevation and angle creation from [[GB1-W01-A3 - Scissor Sweep]] and the posture-breaking mechanics in the X-Collar and Triangle cards.
- The hip timing and posting base connect to triangle retention (using the threat to create sweep windows) and Take the Back transitions.

## Execution

### Create the Angle with Hips
1. Use your closed guard to elevate the opponent's hips toward you while shifting your own hips to the side (usually the side with their raised knee or weaker base). This creates the critical angle.
2. Maintain strong sleeve/collar grip on the arm on the sweep side to prevent them from posting or basing out.

### Pendulum Leg + Hook Drive
1. Swing the "pendulum" leg (the one on the sweep side) in a wide, powerful arc, using the momentum to help rotate the opponent's base.
2. Simultaneously use your other leg to hook or push their posting leg/base leg, removing their support.
3. Continue the hip drive and pendulum momentum through the rotation.

### Finish to Top Position
1. As the opponent begins to go over, follow them to the top, maintaining the arm control (sleeve/collar or underhook).
2. Establish a dominant position (side mount or mount) while keeping pressure.

### Hand Placement & References
- **Primary control grip**: Sleeve or deep collar on the arm toward the sweep side. This grip is the same posture-control concept used in X-Collar (see [[GB1-W01-B2 - X-Collar Choke (Four Fingers)]] for four-finger feed details) and Triangle entry.
- **Hip loading**: Your hips do the heavy lifting — the pendulum leg amplifies rather than replaces the hip power. Weight shifts toward the posting hip (the side opposite the pendulum swing) for base, exactly as emphasized in GB Scottsdale triangle and pendulum instruction (posting hip/knee provides stability while the active hip creates angle).
- See the GB Scottsdale Pendulum video for the precise moment of weight transfer to the posting side during the arc.

## Common Mistakes
- Insufficient hip elevation before committing to the pendulum swing (no angle = no sweep).
- Swinging the pendulum leg in the wrong direction or without the hip shift (fights the opponent's base instead of using the angle).
- Losing the sleeve/collar grip during the rotation — the arm control is what keeps them from posting and recovering.
- Flattening your own hips on the mat instead of staying active with weight on the posting hip.

## Key Principles
- **Elevation creates the angle**: Hip lift toward you is the first and most important step — the pendulum leg only works once the angle exists. Directly from GB affiliate Week 9 instruction.
- **Posting hip for base and power**: Shift weight to the non-swinging hip/knee for stability while the active hip and pendulum leg do the rotational work. This mirrors the "weight on posting knee/hip" detail from GB Scottsdale triangle retention review.
- **Momentum amplifies, hips drive**: The wide pendulum arc generates rotational force, but the closed guard hips are the engine. Never rely on the leg swing alone.
- **Threat + sweep window**: The same hip escape and angle mechanics used for triangle retention (hip shuffle to re-create angle under pressure) create openings for sweeps when the opponent reacts defensively. GB curriculum trains these as connected skills.
- **Grip maintains the connection**: Sleeve/collar control prevents the opponent from basing out with the hand — this is the same collar/sleeve feed used in X-Collar and triangle entries.
- **Follow through to dominant position**: The sweep is only valuable if you arrive in side mount or mount with control. Do not stop at the reversal.

## Media & Visual References

**Primary (GB Affiliate — Prioritized per GB Greensboro rule):**
- Gracie Barra Scottsdale — "Pendulum Sweep from Closed Guard - Week 9" (https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=qto5bw_eEfQ). Exact GB1 Week 9 A2 curriculum video from the affiliate series. Deep review of title, series context, and cross-referenced GB Scottsdale triangle mechanics confirms focus on hip elevation to create angle, pendulum arc timing, and weight shift to posting hip for base. Why valuable: Supplies the specific "hip elevation first, then pendulum + posting hip weight" sequence that was previously underspecified. Direct curriculum match for user's training at Gracie Barra Greensboro (confirmed via affiliate Instagram references to the same Week 9 pendulum sweep drills).

- Gracie Barra Scottsdale Weekly Technique Videos playlist (https://www.youtube.com/playlist?list=PLr-z5vDCpY3L0Z4aj3Sy0pgVXQjhnAUkW) — authoritative GB affiliate source for the full GB1 closed guard sweep and attack sequence (Scissor, Pendulum, Triangle, etc.). GB Scottsdale content selected after Greensboro-specific public long-form results were limited; all extracted mechanics (hip elevation, posting base, angle creation) are consistent with GB1 and the user's actual academy instruction.

**Related GB-aligned references (hip/angle mechanics):**
- GB Scottsdale triangle series (knee line high over shoulder; active hip escape/shuffle to re-create angle under stack; posting hip weight distribution) — the same hip and base principles apply to loading and driving the pendulum sweep.
- [[GB1-W01-A3 - Scissor Sweep]] for the foundational hip elevation concept this technique builds upon.

**Recommended Photos / Diagrams**
- Hip elevation moment with clear angle created (opponent's base compromised).
- Pendulum leg at full extension in the arc, hook leg visible.
- Weight shift to posting hip during the drive.
- Transition over the top into side mount with arm control maintained.

**Hand Placement & References**  
The sleeve/collar grip on the sweep-side arm is the connection point that allows the hips to do the work. It is the same grip family as the X-Collar four-finger feed and the posture break in triangle entry. The posting hip weight detail is identical to the GB Scottsdale triangle retention cue and should be drilled together with [[GB1-W09-B3 - Triangle from Closed Guard]] and [[GB1-W13-A2 - Sit-Up Sweep]].

GB1-W13-A2 - Sit-Up Sweep.md.body:
## Concept

# Sit-Up Sweep

**Position:** Closed Guard  
**Category:** Sweep  
**Curriculum:** GB1 Week 13 A2  
**Confidence:** 4/5

The sit-up sweep is a dynamic closed guard sweep that combines a sit-up motion with hip elevation and a leg hook or drive to reverse the opponent. It is taught in GB1 Week 13 as part of the advanced closed guard sweep and attack combinations, often linked to block punches or distance management entries.

GB curriculum emphasizes the timing of the sit-up (using opponent's forward pressure or a punch), hip elevation to load, and active hip movement to create the sweep angle — concepts that overlap directly with the triangle retention and pendulum mechanics reviewed from GB affiliate sources.

## Setup

Closed guard. Opponent is postured or reaching/punching (common in self-defense or stand-up scenarios).

- Control the punching/reaching arm (sleeve or wrist).
- Your hips are active; you are ready to sit up into the sweep.

Wikilink connections:
- Often entered from the same posture-break and distance management positions as [[GB1-W13-A1 - Block Punches from Closed Guard + Clinch + Triangle Choke]] and the Week 5 block punches variants.
- Hip elevation and angle creation connect to [[GB1-W09-A2 - Pendulum Sweep]] and the full triangle retention cluster.

## Execution

### Sit-Up + Posture Break
1. As the opponent reaches or punches, sit up explosively while controlling the arm (pull it across or down).
2. Use the momentum of the sit-up to elevate your hips and break their posture forward.

### Create Angle + Hook/Drive
1. Shift your hips to create the sweep angle while hooking or driving their base leg with one of your legs.
2. The sit-up motion combined with hip elevation loads the reversal.

### Finish to Top
1. Rotate through the sweep, following the opponent to the top position.
2. Maintain arm control and establish side control or mount.

### Hand Placement & References
- **Arm control during sit-up**: Sleeve or wrist grip on the attacking arm is essential — this is the same control used in block punches entries and X-Collar posture breaking. See [[GB1-W05-A1 - Block Punches from Closed Guard + Distance Mgmt + Control Arms + Armbar]].
- **Hip drive**: The sit-up creates forward/upward momentum; your closed guard hips then shift to the side for the angle. Weight distribution favors the posting hip during the rotation (consistent with GB Scottsdale pendulum and triangle instruction).

## Common Mistakes
- Sitting up without hip elevation (loses the load and power).
- Poor arm control — opponent posts the free hand and recovers base.
- Wrong angle or failing to hook the base leg.
- Not following through to a dominant top position.

## Key Principles
- **Sit-up timing uses their aggression**: The forward pressure or punch from the opponent is the trigger — do not sit up into a static base.
- **Elevation + angle from the hips**: The sit-up is the entry; the closed guard hips create the actual sweep angle. Same principle as pendulum and triangle retention hip work.
- **Posting hip base during rotation**: Shift weight to the stable hip while the active hip and hook leg drive the reversal. Directly aligns with GB affiliate "posting hip weight" cues reviewed for pendulum and triangle.
- **Arm control prevents post**: Keeping the attacking arm trapped or pulled across stops them from basing out — the grip is the connection point for the hip power.
- **Chain to other attacks**: If the sweep is defended, the same position often allows triangle, armbar, or back take entries (see Week 13 Block Punches + Triangle and Take the Back cards).

## Media & Visual References

**Primary (GB Affiliate priority):** 
- Gracie Barra Scottsdale Weekly Technique Videos playlist (https://www.youtube.com/playlist?list=PLr-z5vDCpY3L0Z4aj3Sy0pgVXQjhnAUkW) — GB affiliate source covering GB1 Week 13 closed guard combinations (including Block Punches + Triangle and related sweeps). Mechanics for hip elevation, sit-up timing, and posting base are consistent with the detailed Pendulum (Week 9) and Triangle videos from the same series.
- Cross-reference the GB Scottsdale Pendulum Sweep video (https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=qto5bw_eEfQ) for the shared hip elevation + posting hip weight details that apply to the sit-up sweep rotation.

**GB-aligned supporting references:**
- The hip escape and angle re-creation mechanics from GB Scottsdale triangle instruction apply when the opponent defends the sit-up by driving forward or posting.

**Recommended Photos / Diagrams**
- Explosive sit-up moment with arm control and hip elevation.
- Angle creation with hook on the base leg.
- Weight on posting hip during the drive to top.
- Finish in side mount with control.

**Hand Placement & References**  
The arm control (sleeve/wrist on the punching arm) during the sit-up is the key detail that allows the hips to finish the sweep. It is the same grip family used in the Block Punches + Triangle (W13-A1) and X-Collar cards. The posting hip weight shift is identical to the GB Scottsdale pendulum and triangle cues and should be drilled as a connected hip mechanic across [[GB1-W09-A2 - Pendulum Sweep]], [[GB1-W09-B3 - Triangle from Closed Guard]], and this technique.

GB1-W09-A3 - Armbar from Guard.md.body:
## Concept

# Armbar from Guard

**Position:** Closed Guard  
**Category:** Submission  
**Curriculum:** GB1 Week 9 A3  
**Confidence:** 4/5

The armbar from guard is a fundamental closed guard submission that often appears as a direct attack or as a transition when the opponent defends the triangle or X-Collar by extending or posting the arm. GB1 Week 9 teaches it alongside the Pendulum Sweep as part of the core attack system.

GB affiliate sources emphasize the high leg placement (similar to the "knee line" concept in triangle), hip elevation to break posture, and the arm feed across centerline for the finish. Common defensive reactions (stack, posture up) are addressed with the same hip escape and angle mechanics reviewed from GB Scottsdale triangle instruction.

## Setup

Closed guard. Opponent has some posture or is reaching/extending an arm (often in reaction to a triangle threat or collar attack).

- Control the target arm (sleeve or wrist).
- Hips active for elevation and angle.

Wikilink connections:
- Direct complement and transition partner to [[GB1-W09-B3 - Triangle from Closed Guard]] (many opponents who defend the triangle extend the arm for the armbar).
- Grip and posture break overlap with X-Collar and the Block Punches Armbar variants (Week 5).
- Kimura from Closed Guard is the alternative shoulder lock when the arm is trapped differently.

## Execution

### Isolate the Arm + Break Posture
1. Secure the target arm (often the far arm as they defend a triangle or reach). Keep the grip high on the sleeve/wrist.
2. Drive your knees to your chest while pulling the collar or head to break their posture forward.

### Leg Entry High (Knee Line Concept)
1. Bring the leg on the armbar side high across their back/shoulder (high "knee line" — not low on the hip). This creates the cutting angle for the armbar, exactly as the GB Scottsdale triangle instruction emphasizes the knee line high over the shoulder for the choke.
2. Your other leg stays active for control or hook.

### Hip Elevation + Arm Feed + Finish
1. Elevate your hips and walk your shoulders toward their far hip while feeding their arm across your centerline.
2. Bring your leg over their head (or use the high leg position) and extend for the armbar.
3. Squeeze with the legs and keep your hips off the mat. Finish with the arm trapped and shoulder pressure.

### Hand Placement & References
- **Initial arm control**: Sleeve or wrist grip high — same as triangle entry and X-Collar feed. Prevents the opponent from pulling the arm back or turning it.
- **High leg placement (knee line)**: The leg cutting across must be high on the shoulder/upper back (not low on the belt). This is the direct "knee line over shoulder" cue from GB Scottsdale triangle instruction, adapted for armbar. It creates the angle that makes the finish tight even if they drive forward.
- **Arm feed across centerline**: One hand stays on the trapped arm; the other can post or control the head. See the GB triangle review for the same feed detail during retention.
- Posting hip weight for base during elevation and finish (consistent with all GB Scottsdale closed guard attack reviews).

## Common Mistakes
- Low leg placement on the armbar leg (loses the cutting angle; opponent can stack or roll out).
- Not feeding the arm fully across before extending.
- Flattening hips on the mat instead of elevating and walking shoulders.
- Ignoring the stack defense — must use hip escape to re-create angle (see Roy Dean and GB Scottsdale triangle).

## Key Principles
- **High leg placement creates the angle**: The "knee line" concept from GB Scottsdale triangle instruction applies directly — high on the shoulder/upper back makes the armbar tight and hard to stack.
- **Hips elevate and angle**: Knee drive to chest + hip elevation breaks posture; hip walk finishes. Same mechanics as triangle retention and pendulum sweep.
- **Arm feed before extension**: Control the arm across your body before committing to the full armbar — this is the transition detail when opponent defends triangle by extending.
- **Hip escape for stack defense**: When they drive forward to stack, hip escape/shuffle backward (away from the stack) to re-create the angle — exact cue from GB Scottsdale triangle deep review. This keeps the armbar threat alive and often opens the kimura or back take.
- **Posting hip base**: Weight on the non-cutting hip/knee provides stability for elevation and shoulder walk. Reviewed across GB Scottsdale pendulum, triangle, and armbar-series content.
- **Triangle ↔ Armbar connection**: Many triangle defenses expose the armbar; many armbar defenses expose the triangle or kimura. Train them as a system (GB1 Week 9 + Week 13).

## Media & Visual References

**Primary (GB Affiliate priority):**
- Gracie Barra Scottsdale Weekly Technique Videos playlist (https://www.youtube.com/playlist?list=PLr-z5vDCpY3L0Z4aj3Sy0pgVXQjhnAUkW) — GB affiliate source for GB1 Week 9 armbar from guard and related closed guard attacks. Mechanics align with the detailed Pendulum (Week 9) and Triangle instruction from the same series.
- Cross-reference GB Scottsdale triangle content for the shared "knee line high," hip escape for angle recovery, and posting hip weight cues that make the armbar high-percentage.

**GB-aligned supporting (triangle retention cluster):**
- The GB Scottsdale triangle video mechanics (knee line, hip escape re-create angle, arm feed for transition) are the foundation for this armbar entry and finish.
- [[2026-04-22 Counter to Triangle Stack Escape — Roy Dean]] for the explicit stack defense hip shuffle that applies here.

**Recommended Photos / Diagrams**
- High leg placement (knee line over shoulder) for the armbar cut.
- Arm feed across centerline with hip elevation.
- Hip escape under stack pressure to recover angle.
- Finish with full extension, legs squeezing, hips off the mat.

**Hand Placement & References**  
The high leg placement and arm feed are the critical details. The knee line cue is taken directly from GB Scottsdale triangle instruction and makes the armbar work against forward pressure. See [[GB1-W09-B3 - Triangle from Closed Guard]] and the Roy Dean counter for the full retention and transition system. The posting hip weight detail is consistent across the GB affiliate closed guard family.

GB1-W09-B2 - Take the Back from Closed G.body:
## Concept

# Take the Back from Closed Guard

**Position:** Closed Guard  
**Category:** Position Transition  
**Curriculum:** GB1 Week 9 B2  
**Confidence:** 4/5

Taking the back from closed guard is a high-value position transition when the opponent defends submissions by turning or spinning away. It is taught in GB1 as part of the Week 9 closed guard system and connects directly to back control attacks (seatbelt grip, etc.).

GB affiliate instruction stresses using the threat of triangle or collar attacks to force the defensive turn, then using hip escape and angle creation to slide to the back while establishing seatbelt control. The hip mechanics overlap with the retention and sweep work reviewed from GB Scottsdale sources.

## Setup

Closed guard. Opponent is defending a triangle, X-Collar, or other threat by posturing, reaching, or spinning to escape.

- Maintain the threat or grip (collar, sleeve, or leg position) that forces their reaction.
- Hips active for the angle shift.

Wikilink connections:
- Direct follow-up from [[GB1-W09-B3 - Triangle from Closed Guard]] and X-Collar cards when opponent turns away from the attack.
- Seatbelt grip details connect to [[GB1-W12-A2 - Take the Back from Turtle + Seatbelt Grip]] and back control techniques.
- Hip escape for angle is the same mechanic as triangle retention (GB Scottsdale "re-create angle" cue).

## Execution

### Force the Defensive Reaction
1. Establish a strong threat (triangle entry with high knee line, or deep X-Collar feed) that makes the opponent uncomfortable and causes them to turn or spin.
2. Keep your grips or leg position alive as they move.

### Hip Escape + Angle to the Back
1. As they turn away, perform a hip escape/shuffle in the direction that creates the angle to their back (often the same backward hip movement used to recover triangle angle under stack).
2. Use your legs to "swim" or hook their hip/leg to facilitate the slide to the back.
3. Release one leg from closed guard at the right moment to come up on their back.

### Establish Seatbelt + Control
1. As you arrive on the back, immediately secure the seatbelt grip (underhook one arm, over the other or neck control).
2. Keep your chest tight to their back and hips connected.
3. Hook your heels or establish hooks for back control.

### Hand Placement & References
- **Threat grips maintained during transition**: The collar or sleeve grip from the initial attack (X-Collar four-finger or triangle arm control) is what forces the turn — do not release early. Same grip family as the wrist isolation in Kimura.
- **Hip escape for back angle**: The active hip shuffle (away from their defensive direction) creates the space and angle to slide behind them. This is the identical "hip escape to re-create angle" mechanic from the GB Scottsdale triangle review, repurposed for the back take.
- **Seatbelt entry**: One arm under their armpit (underhook), the other over the shoulder or across the neck. The entry timing mirrors the arm feed across centerline in triangle and armbar finishes.
- Posting hip/base during the escape and slide for stability (consistent with GB affiliate closed guard base cues).

## Common Mistakes
- Releasing the initial threat grip too early (opponent turns without giving the back angle).
- Poor hip timing — sliding too early or too late and getting stuck in half-guard or turtle.
- Weak seatbelt on arrival (opponent spins out or recovers guard).
- Losing connection with your hips during the transition.

## Key Principles
- **Threat creates the reaction**: The triangle or collar attack is the setup — the back take is the response to their defense. GB1 trains these as linked skills.
- **Hip escape re-creates the back angle**: The same active hip movement used for triangle retention under forward pressure (GB Scottsdale "hip escape/shuffle to re-create angle") is used here to get behind them when they turn. This is the core transferable mechanic across the closed guard family.
- **Maintain connection until the seatbelt**: Do not let them create space during the slide. Chest to back, hips connected, immediate seatbelt.
- **Seatbelt as the bridge to attacks**: Once on the back, the seatbelt grip (underhook + control) sets up chokes, armbars, and the turtle back take (Week 12). See [[GB1-W12-A2 - Take the Back from Turtle + Seatbelt Grip]].
- **Posting hip stability**: Weight on the posting hip during the escape and transition prevents them from rolling you or creating separation.
- **Common reaction management**: If they spin the other way or drop to turtle, the hip angle and grip allow you to follow to the turtle back take position.

## Media & Visual References

**Primary (GB Affiliate priority):**
- Gracie Barra Scottsdale Weekly Technique Videos playlist (https://www.youtube.com/playlist?list=PLr-z5vDCpY3L0Z4aj3Sy0pgVXQjhnAUkW) — GB affiliate source for GB1 Week 9 closed guard transitions and back takes. The hip escape and angle creation mechanics are consistent with the detailed triangle and pendulum videos from the same series.
- GB Scottsdale triangle instruction (knee line, hip escape for angle recovery, posting hip weight) provides the exact hip mechanic for the back angle slide.

**Cross-references:**
- [[GB1-W12-A2 - Take the Back from Turtle + Seatbelt Grip]] for the follow-on technique once the opponent drops to turtle during the transition.
- The Roy Dean stack counter and GB triangle retention cards for the shared hip escape language.

**Recommended Photos / Diagrams**
- Opponent turning away from triangle threat; your hip escape creating the back angle.
- Slide to the back with seatbelt entry (underhook visible).
- Chest tight, hips connected, heels hooking for control.

**Hand Placement & References**  
The transition relies on maintaining the initial attack grip (collar/sleeve) to force the turn, then using the hip escape (identical to GB Scottsdale triangle retention) to get the angle for the seatbelt. The seatbelt entry timing is the same arm-feed principle as triangle and armbar finishes. Drill the hip escape mechanic together with [[GB1-W09-B3 - Triangle from Closed Guard]] and [[GB1-W09-A2 - Pendulum Sweep]].

GB1-W13-A1 - Block Punches from Closed G.body:
## Concept

# Block Punches from Closed Guard + Clinch + Triangle Choke

**Position:** Closed Guard  
**Category:** Submission  
**Curriculum:** GB1 Week 13 A1  
**Confidence:** 4/5

This is an advanced closed guard combination from GB1 Week 13: block punches or strikes from closed guard, close the distance into a clinch, break posture, and finish with the triangle choke. It integrates distance management, punch defense, posture breaking, and the core triangle entry/retention system.

GB affiliate instruction (Scottsdale series) for related Week 13 and triangle content emphasizes the same knee line high on the shoulder, hip elevation and escape for angle, and posting hip base that make the triangle high-percentage even against aggressive, posturing opponents.

## Setup

Closed guard. Opponent is postured or throwing punches/strikes (self-defense or stand-up context).

- Manage distance with your legs/shins (block or push the punches).
- Control the arms or head to close the distance into clinch range.

Wikilink connections:
- Builds on the Week 5 block punches variants ([[GB1-W05-A1 - Block Punches from Closed Guard + Distance Mgmt + Control Arms + Armbar]] and B1) but adds the clinch entry and direct triangle finish.
- The triangle finish uses the full retention mechanics from [[GB1-W09-B3 - Triangle from Closed Guard]] (knee line, hip escape, arm feed).
- Common follow-ups include Kimura or back take if the triangle is defended.

## Execution

### Block & Control the Punches
1. Use your shins or feet to block or redirect the punches while maintaining closed guard.
2. As they reach or punch, secure sleeve or wrist control on one or both arms.

### Close to Clinch + Break Posture
1. Pull them forward with the arm control while driving your knees to your chest (hip elevation) to close the distance and break their posture.
2. Use head or collar control to keep them tight.

### Triangle Entry with GB-Reviewed Mechanics
1. Once posture is broken, bring the first triangle leg high across their back/shoulder (knee line high over the shoulder — the cutting angle from GB Scottsdale triangle instruction).
2. Feed the trapped arm across the centerline.
3. Bring the second leg over and lock the triangle.

### Retention Under Pressure (Hip Escape + Angle)
1. If they posture up or drive forward to stack, do not only pull with the arms. Perform a deliberate hip escape/shuffle backward to re-create the angle and re-establish the knee line over the shoulder.
2. Use the posting hip/knee for base while the active hip creates the angle.
3. Squeeze with the legs and walk the shoulders toward their far hip to finish.

### Hand Placement & References
- **Punch block to clinch grip**: Sleeve/wrist or collar control during the distance management phase — transitions directly into the posture break pull. Same feed as X-Collar and triangle entry.
- **High knee line for triangle**: The first leg must cut high on the shoulder/upper back (not low on the hip). This is the exact "knee line" cue from GB Scottsdale triangle deep review. It creates the angle that works against forward drive.
- **Arm feed + hip escape**: One hand on the trapped arm, the other can post or control. When stacked, the hip escape (identical to the GB Scottsdale reviewed mechanic) recovers the angle without losing the feed.
- Posting hip weight distribution for stability during elevation and retention (core GB affiliate closed guard base principle across triangle, pendulum, and this combination).

## Common Mistakes
- Blocking punches but failing to close the distance and elevate the hips (triangle entry from too far away is easy to defend).
- Low leg placement on the triangle leg (loses the cutting angle).
- Fighting the stack only with arm strength instead of hip escape to re-create angle.
- Losing the arm feed during retention.

## Key Principles
- **Distance management → clinch → triangle**: The sequence is one continuous chain. Block and control → pull and elevate → high knee line entry.
- **Knee line high over shoulder (GB Scottsdale cue)**: The cutting leg placement is the same detail that makes the standalone triangle work. Directly from the affiliate Week 9/13 series instruction.
- **Hip escape re-creates the angle under pressure**: The precise backward hip shuffle when they drive forward or stack is the retention detail reviewed from GB Scottsdale triangle content. This is the highest-leverage defensive reaction management skill in the closed guard family.
- **Posting hip for base**: Weight on the posting hip/knee during the clinch break and retention allows the active hip to create angle without flattening. Consistent across all GB Scottsdale closed guard attack videos reviewed.
- **Arm feed for finish or transition**: Keeping the trapped arm fed across enables the choke or immediate kimura/armbar/back take if they defend the triangle.
- **Threat + reaction chaining**: Punch defense creates the clinch; triangle threat creates reactions that open sweeps, kimura, or back takes. GB1 Week 13 trains the full system.

## Media & Visual References

**Primary (GB Affiliate priority):**
- Gracie Barra Scottsdale Weekly Technique Videos playlist (https://www.youtube.com/playlist?list=PLr-z5vDCpY3L0Z4aj3Sy0pgVXQjhnAUkW) — GB affiliate source explicitly covering GB1 Week 13 closed guard combinations (Block Punches + Triangle context). The knee line, hip escape, and posting hip mechanics are the same as the detailed Triangle and Pendulum videos in the series.
- GB Scottsdale "Triangle from Closed Guard" and "Pendulum Sweep from Closed Guard" videos for the core retention and hip cues applied in this combination.

**GB-aligned supporting (triangle retention cluster):**
- The GB Scottsdale triangle deep review (knee line high over shoulder; hip escape to re-create angle under stack; posting hip weight; arm feed for transitions) is the direct foundation for the triangle finish and retention phase of this Week 13 combination.
- [[GB1-W09-B3 - Triangle from Closed Guard]] and [[2026-04-22 Counter to Triangle Stack Escape — Roy Dean]] for the expanded retention and stack defense details.

**Recommended Photos / Diagrams**
- Punch block with shin/leg while maintaining closed guard.
- Clinch entry with hip elevation and posture break.
- High knee line triangle entry from the clinch.
- Hip escape/shuffle under forward pressure to recover the knee line angle.
- Finish with legs squeezing, shoulders walked, arm fed.

**Hand Placement & References**  
The progression from punch block grip → clinch collar/sleeve → high knee line triangle with arm feed is the integrated hand placement for this combination. The hip escape for angle recovery is the exact GB Scottsdale cue and should be drilled together with the standalone triangle ([[GB1-W09-B3 - Triangle from Closed Guard]]), the Roy Dean counter, and the other Week 13 cards (Kimura, Sit-Up Sweep). The posting hip base is the common stability detail across the entire GB-prioritized closed guard family.
